description: Medical phrases for doctor visits, symptoms, and health situations

items.english: I have an appointment with the doctor.
items.notes: 'Aika' here means 'appointment' (also means 'time'). 'Lääkärille' is the allative case of 'lääkäri' (doctor). In Finland, you typically need to book appointments through a health center ('terveyskeskus') or private clinic.
Basic statement of illness. 'Sairas' is an adjective meaning 'sick/ill'. This is often the first thing you tell the doctor or nurse when arriving.

'Kuumetta' is the partitive form of 'kuume' (fever). The partitive is used because fever is an ongoing condition. Normal body temperature is 37°C in Finland. Fever ('kuume') is typically considered to start at 38°C.

'Päätäni' is the partitive of 'pää' (head) with possessive suffix '-ni'. 'Särkee' is an impersonal verb meaning 'aches/hurts'. This construction 'X:ää särkee' is the Finnish way to express body pain. You can replace 'päätäni' with other body parts.
'Yskää' is the partitive form of 'yskä' (cough). Same possessive construction as other symptoms. You can add 'kuivaa yskää' (dry cough) or 'limayskää' (phlegmy cough) for more detail.
'Vatsavaivoja' is the partitive plural of 'vatsavaiva' (stomach trouble), a compound from 'vatsa' (stomach) + 'vaiva' (ailment). This is a polite, general way to describe digestive issues.
'Allerginen' takes the allative case (-lle) for the allergen. This is critical medical information. Always inform your doctor of allergies. You can replace 'penisilliinille' with other allergens.

'Reseptin' is the genitive/accusative of 'resepti' (prescription). In Finland, prescriptions are electronic ('e-resepti') and sent directly to the pharmacy system. You just show your ID at any pharmacy.

'Lähin' is the superlative of 'lähellä' (near). 'Apteekki' means 'pharmacy'. Finnish pharmacies are marked with a green cross. Many common medicines require a prescription in Finland that may be over-the-counter elsewhere.

items.english: How often do I take the medicine?
items.notes: 'Kuinka usein' means 'how often'. 'Otan' is the first person of 'ottaa' (to take). 'Lääkettä' is the partitive of 'lääke' (medicine). Common answers: 'kerran päivässä' (once a day), 'kahdesti päivässä' (twice a day).

items.english: I need to take a blood test.
items.notes: 'Minun pitää' means 'I must/need to'. 'Verikoe' is a compound word from 'veri' (blood) + 'koe' (test). Blood tests in Finland are often done at a laboratory ('laboratorio'), which may be at the health center or hospital.

'Sairaslomaa' is the partitive of 'sairasloma' (sick leave), a compound from 'sairas' (sick) + 'loma' (leave). In Finland, a doctor's certificate ('lääkärintodistus') is usually required for sick leave beyond 3 days.
'Vakavaa' is the partitive of 'vakava' (serious). The partitive is used here because the question concerns the nature/degree of the condition. A natural question to ask your doctor about a diagnosis.

'Kuinka kauan' means 'how long (duration)'. 'Paraneminen' means 'recovery/healing' from 'parantua' (to recover). 'Kestää' means 'to last/take (time)'. Common answers include 'muutama päivä' (a few days) or 'pari viikkoa' (a couple of weeks).
'Milloin' means 'when'. 'Tulen' is the first person of 'tulla' (to come). 'Uudestaan' means 'again'. Ask this to understand follow-up appointment scheduling.

'Sattuuko' is from 'sattua' (to hurt) with the question suffix '-ko'. This is what the doctor might ask you during an examination. Answer 'Kyllä, sattuu' (Yes, it hurts) or 'Ei satu' (No, it doesn't hurt).

'Minua' is the partitive of 'minä'. 'Huimaa' is an impersonal verb meaning 'to feel dizzy'. This impersonal construction (partitive person + verb) is typical for Finnish body sensations. There is no separate subject.
'Oksettaa' is an impersonal verb meaning 'to feel nauseous'. Same impersonal construction as 'huimaa'. If you have vomited, you can say 'Olen oksentanut' (I have vomited).

'Hammaslääkärin' is the genitive/accusative of 'hammaslääkäri' (dentist), a compound from 'hammas' (tooth) + 'lääkäri' (doctor). Public dental care is available through health centers, but private clinics often have shorter wait times.

'Näköni' is 'näkö' (vision) with possessive suffix '-ni' (my). 'Heikentynyt' is the past participle of 'heikentyä' (to weaken/worsen). Use this at an optician ('optikko') or eye doctor ('silmälääkäri') appointment.
